text: The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection of the sigmoid colon, navigating around the superior hemorrhoidal pedicle, which is adhered to the sidewall due to the patient's size.
The surgeon unfolds large epiploic appendages to expose the anti-mesenteric surface of the bowel during the robotic sigmoid resection.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection of the sigmoid colon, attempting to mobilize the area enough to elevate the rectosigmoid junction and access the superior hemorrhoidal pedicle, though this remains challenging due to the patient's anatomy.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and continues lateral mobilization of the bowel, preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery due to good bowel mobility and benign disease.
The surgeon selects the proximal and distal margins of the bowel for resection, considering a transrectal extraction of the specimen to avoid an extraction incision in the large-sized patient.

The surgeon performs a Pfannenstiel incision for specimen extraction, having decided against transrectal extraction due to difficulty in placing the ring clamp transanally and concerns about potential rectal damage.
The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery at the level of the intended proximal margin, marking the medial edge and scoring it down towards the distal margin at the rectosigmoid junction.

The surgeon proceeds to dissect and take the mesentery, which appears floppy and fatty, while maneuvering around the colon. The dissection is challenging, but progresses without the use of an assist port.

The surgeon cleans the recto sigmoid junction, mobilizing around the limited splenic flexure without needing to re-dock or change trocar positions. The dissection is performed to gain additional length, with visible manipulation of the bowel.
The surgeon elevates the descending colon mesentery away from the kidney, proceeding to remove omental adhesions in the left upper quadrant, which provides additional length for the procedure.

The surgeon checks both margins of the colon, ensuring they are clean, and performs a perfusion assessment. The colon reaches the pelvis without difficulty. The bowel wall is transected using a vessel sealer, followed by transection of the proximal margin.

The surgeon places the resected bowel specimen to the side and begins a single-layer sewn anastomosis. A lateral suture is used for traction, while full-thickness bites are taken starting at the posterior midline.
The surgeon checks the perfusion of the bowel and begins sewing the anastomosis on the left side, using a silk suture for support.

The surgeon switches the suture to the outside, preparing to canal, and begins placing the second suture starting in the posterior midline. The suture is then positioned anteriorly as part of the single-layer anastomosis technique.

The surgeon uses the original suture to continue hand-sewn anastomosis, carefully placing each suture bite despite limited visibility. The surgeon demonstrates confidence in the security of the sutures despite the challenging visual conditions.

The surgeon ties two sutures together at the anastomosis site, then runs an additional suture towards the original stay suture for added security. The pelvis is flooded for a leak test, and a rigid proctosigmoidoscopy confirms there is no leak.
